property.narrative:
2 Clover Lane is a 4-bedroom, 2.5-bath (2 full + 1 half) Colonial built in 1992, with 1,976 sq ft of above-grade finished living area, an unfinished basement, and 8 total rooms, sitting on a 1.59-acre lot (MBL F15 120 003, zone R-40, Single Family). Current owners are the current owners, who bought it 12/16/2020 for $339,000 (prior sale 8/11/2000 for $236,000). Per Simsbury's online assessor (data updated 6/5/2026), the 2022-revaluation appraised value is $406,300 (Land $162,800 + Buildings $243,500) and the assessed value (70%) is $284,410. At the current 2025 Grand List total mill rate of 35.00 (Town 33.67 + Fire 1.33), the annual real estate tax is about $9,954 (it was ~$9,716 at the prior 34.16 rate). Current market estimates run roughly $546K (Trulia/Zillow) to ~$618K (Movoto), broadly consistent with a Simsbury market where the typical home value is ~$449K and up ~7.4% year over year; the assessor's $406K appraised figure lags the live market because it reflects 2022 conditions.

KEY DECISION-RELEVANT FINDING: This home is ALREADY in the Latimer Lane Elementary attendance zone. The official Simsbury Public Schools elementary street listing (Revised 4/23/2025) lists "Clover Lane" under the "Latimer" district. That removes "getting into the Latimer Lane zone" as a reason to sell and buy elsewhere — the family already has it. The decision therefore reduces primarily to a space question: the current house is small (1,976 sq ft) for a family of five with three young children, but the 1.59-acre lot is roughly 1.7x the R-40 minimum and leaves room to add on. R-40 setbacks (front 50 ft, side 40 ft, rear 50 ft, height 35 ft) mean a rear addition is the most feasible path; the binding side-yard distance and exact buildable area should be confirmed against a plot plan/survey and the town GIS before scoping an addition. For the build-vs-buy-vs-wait analysis, this means Option A (addition) keeps the desired school zone and a low ~$284K tax basis, whereas Option B (buy a larger home in the Latimer zone for $500K-$700K+) would reset to a much higher assessment/tax and likely a higher rate environment.
caveats: Estimator portals (Zillow, Redfin, Trulia, ecode360) blocked direct WebFetch (403); the market estimate of ~$545,800 (Trulia/Zillow) comes from a search-result snippet and was not loaded from the live page, and Movoto's $618,000 came via a successful fetch. Treat market value as a ~$540K-$620K range, not a precise figure.
The Trulia/Movoto pages show baths as '3' while the town assessor record shows 2 full + 1 half (2.5); the assessor's 2.5 is the authoritative figure.
Movoto's 'last sold' showed the older 8/11/2000 $236,000 sale and missed the 12/16/2020 $339,000 sale; the assessor sales history (used here) is authoritative and shows both.

Assessed value reflects the 2022 town-wide revaluation; Simsbury's next revaluation will reset this. Mill rate 35.00 is the 2025 Grand List rate (bills mailed ~6/29/2026); confirm the fire-district component applicable to this specific parcel as Simsbury has multiple fire districts.

Exact maximum lot/building coverage percentage for R-40 was not captured from the zoning PDF (table likely image-based); verify with Simsbury Planning/Zoning. R-40 dimensional setbacks (lot 40,000 sf, frontage 200 ft, front 50 ft, side 40 ft, rear 50 ft, height 35 ft) came from a search summary of the regs and should be confirmed against the current adopted regulations.

Addition feasibility is directional; a definitive answer requires the structure's measured setbacks from each lot line (survey/plot plan), wetlands/septic/well location (this is a well/septic area), and a Planning & Zoning consult.

Simsbury lists a 'Central' elementary district alongside Latimer/Squadron/Tariffville/Tootin' Hills and has had facilities-reconfiguration discussions; attendance boundaries can change, so re-verify the Latimer assignment with the district before relying on it long-term.

schools.narrative:
CRITICAL FINDING: 2 Clover Lane is ALREADY in the Latimer Lane Elementary attendance zone. The authoritative Simsbury Public Schools "Elementary Districts" street listing (revised 4/23/2025) lists "Clover Lane — Latimer." (Latimer Lane School itself sits on Mountain View Road in Weatogue, also listed as "Latimer.") This reframes the family's decision: moving elsewhere in Simsbury to "get into the Latimer Lane zone" is NOT a school upgrade — the family is already in that zone today. So on the schools dimension, Option A (build an addition at 2 Clover Lane) and Option C (wait) keep them in the exact same top-tier elementary at zero risk, while Option B (sell and buy) only makes sense for the schools if they specifically buy another Latimer-zoned home (or accept a different Simsbury elementary). Any move to a different Simsbury elementary zone would, at best, be a lateral move and in most cases a slight step down by the published metrics.

LATIMER LANE PROFILE: Latimer Lane (K-6, ~468 students, ~15:1 ratio) is the single highest-rated elementary in Simsbury by the most recent rankings. GreatSchools 9/10. U.S. News (2024 Best Elementary Schools) ranked it #1 in Simsbury and #33 in Connecticut. PublicSchoolReview (2026 data) puts it at #36 of 939 CT schools (top ~5%): 78% math proficiency (vs ~41% CT avg) and 82% reading proficiency (vs ~50% CT avg). Demographics ~30% minority, ~15% economically disadvantaged. By every published measure it is an excellent, high-performing school.

HOW THE FIVE SIMSBURY ELEMENTARIES COMPARE (most recent GreatSchools rating / proficiency, and U.S. News 2024 rank):
- Latimer Lane: GS 9/10, 78% math / 82% reading; U.S. News #1 in town, #33 CT. (Current zone for 2 Clover Lane.)
- Tootin' Hills (West Simsbury): GS 9/10, 75% math / 77% reading; U.S. News #2 in town, #40 CT. Essentially co-equal with Latimer.
- Central (Simsbury center): GS 8/10, 75% math / 78% reading; U.S. News #3 in town, #73 CT.
- Squadron Line (largest, PK-6, ~700+ students): GS 8-9/10, 69% math / 75% reading; U.S. News #4-5 in town, #108 CT. Lowest economically disadvantaged (~11%).
- Tariffville (smallest, ~263 students): GS 8/10, 62% math / 67% reading; U.S. News #4-5 in town, #130 CT. Still above state average but the lowest of the five and the only one that would be a clear step down from Latimer.

UPGRADE ASSESSMENT: Moving into the Latimer Lane zone would be a "no-op" for this family because they already live there. Relative to the alternatives: a move that LANDED them in Tootin' Hills would be a genuine lateral move (both ~9/10, both top-40 in CT); a move into Central or Squadron Line would be a very slight downgrade; a move into Tariffville would be a more noticeable downgrade. Importantly, ALL FIVE Simsbury elementaries score well above CT averages, so there is no "bad" elementary in town — the differences among them are modest. The strongest schools argument is therefore for STAYING at 2 Clover Lane (addition or wait), since it already secures the town's top elementary at no cost or risk.

DISTRICT QUALITY (all five elementaries feed Henry James Memorial Middle, grades 7-8, then Simsbury High): Simsbury Public Schools is a highly regarded district — ~4,094 students, Niche overall grade A, ranked among the top ~10% of CT districts for overall performance and reading proficiency (district-wide ~66% math / ~76% reading proficient). Henry James Memorial is a former National Blue Ribbon School (GreatSchools 7/10). Simsbury High School is the district crown jewel: GreatSchools 10/10, ~94% graduation rate, multiple College Success Awards, and ranked roughly #14 in Connecticut and ~#530 nationally by U.S. News (2025). The K-12 pipeline is strong regardless of which elementary a Simsbury home is zoned for, which further weakens the case for moving on school grounds alone.
schools.districtSummary: Simsbury Public Schools is a strong, high-performing district (~4,094 students, Niche overall grade A, top ~10% of CT districts; district ~66% math / ~76% reading proficient). All five elementaries score well above CT averages and feed Henry James Memorial Middle (former National Blue Ribbon, GreatSchools 7/10) and then Simsbury High School (GreatSchools 10/10, ~94% grad rate, ~#14 in CT / ~#530 nationally per U.S. News 2025). There is no weak link in the K-12 pipeline.
schools.isLatimerAnUpgrade: No — not an upgrade, because 2 Clover Lane is ALREADY in the Latimer Lane zone. Moving to "get into" Latimer is a non-event for this family. Versus the other Simsbury elementaries, Latimer is the top-rated (GS 9/10, U.S. News #1 in town, #33 in CT); Tootin' Hills is essentially equal, while Central, Squadron Line, and especially Tariffville would each be a slight-to-moderate step down. So the schools argument actually favors STAYING (addition or wait), or only buying another Latimer-zoned (or Tootin' Hills-zoned) home if they move.

Medium on exact proficiency percentages (third-party aggregators, which lag the state by a year and round differently) and on precise U.S. News numeric ranks (rankings shift yearly).
caveats: Zone confirmation is street-name based: the official street listing maps 'Clover Lane' to the Latimer district. The family should still verify with the district registrar (860-651-3361) since a few streets have address-range splits (e.g., Bushy Hill Road, Hopmeadow Street, Farms Village Rd are split by house number) — Clover Lane is NOT split in the listing, so the whole street is Latimer.

Attendance boundaries can be redistricted; Simsbury periodically reviews elementary enrollment balance (Squadron Line is notably larger than the others). A future redistricting could in theory reassign streets, though there is no current indication Clover Lane would change.

Proficiency percentages come from third-party aggregators (GreatSchools, PublicSchoolReview, Niche) that reprocess CT EdSight/Smarter Balanced data; they typically reflect 2022-23 or 2023-24 testing and round differently, so treat them as approximate. The authoritative primary source is CT EdSight (public-edsight.ct.gov), whose interactive Connecticut Report Card I could not fully scrape in this session.
GreatSchools ratings showed minor source-to-source variation for Squadron Line (8 vs 9 out of 10); I report the range.
U.S. News elementary ranks cited are from the 2024 Best Elementary Schools report (article dated Nov 2023); the high school rank is from the 2025 report. Rankings change annually.
Enrollment figures vary slightly by source/year (e.g., Squadron Line cited at ~700-723; Latimer ~468; district ~4,094).

I did not retrieve a single exact district 'Accountability Index' point value from EdSight; district quality is characterized via Niche/U.S. News/GreatSchools instead. If the financial advisor wants the precise state accountability number, pull it directly from CT EdSight's Next Generation Accountability dashboard for Simsbury.

market.narrative:
Snapshot as of early June 2026. Simsbury is a fast-moving, low-inventory seller's market right now, which matters directly to the build-vs-buy-vs-wait decision. Town-wide active inventory is roughly 78-83 single-family units (Movoto/William Pitt), DOWN about 12% year-over-year, and homes are selling fast: median days on market is about 9-14 days (sources range 9 to 14; William Pitt reports 14 days in May 2026, Movoto 9 days). Prices are climbing hard: William Pitt Sotheby's reports a May 2026 median sale price of $675K (+16.4% YoY) and average sale price of $773K (+29.4% YoY), with price per square foot at $282 (+14.3% YoY). Other aggregators are more conservative (Movoto median ~$575K in May 2026; Redfin Simsbury Center ~$223/sqft up 3.5% YoY; Zillow typical value ~$525K up ~5.9% YoY). The spread reflects different geographies (Simsbury Center vs. all 06070/06089/06092) and list vs. sold figures, but every source agrees the direction is up and supply is tight.

For the family's specific goal - 3-4+ bedrooms in the Latimer Lane Elementary zone, $500K-$900K - I used the official Simsbury Public Schools elementary street-to-school listing (rev. 4/23/2025) to classify each property. Importantly, the family's current street, Clover Lane (Weatogue), IS in the Latimer zone, as are nearby Old Meadow Plain Road, Amy Lane, Crane Place, Climax Road, Lawton Drive, Spruce Lane, and Valley View Drive. By contrast, several large listings that show up in 06089/Weatogue searches are NOT Latimer: Tallwood Lane and Long View Drive are Central, Hampshire Lane is Squadron, and West Simsbury (06092) is its own area.

The takeaway on the listing side: genuine Latimer-zone, family-sized homes do exist in budget right now, but the inventory is thin and the best-fit ones skew toward the $480K-$650K band and toward older (1969-1972) homes around 1,700-3,200 sqft. The single strongest currently-active match is 5 Lawton Drive ($649,900, 4bd, 3,157 sqft, 1.07 acres, Latimer zone) - it is comfortably in budget, large enough for five, and in the target school zone. 72 Old Meadow Plain Rd (~$480K-$515K, 4bd/2,193 sqft) is another in-zone active option close to the family's current home. Several other in-zone homes (93 and 98 Old Meadow Plain) recently went under contract within the budget, which itself is evidence of strong demand for exactly this profile.

The trade-off the financial-advisor audience should note: to get a NEWER or LARGER home (5+ beds, 3,000+ sqft) you generally have to leave the Latimer zone (Hampshire Lane, Tallwood, Branch Brook) or stretch toward the top of budget. So within Latimer specifically, a buyer often trades 'newer/bigger' for 'right school zone' - which strengthens the case for evaluating an addition on the existing Clover Lane home (already in-zone) against the relatively limited and fast-selling in-zone purchase options. Given ~9-14 day DOM and falling inventory, the 'wait 1-3 years' option carries real risk that in-zone prices continue rising faster than wages while rates stay elevated.

Use ~$600K as a blended town-wide working figure; Latimer-zone family homes are clustering in the high-$400Ks to mid-$600Ks.

addition.narrative:
ADDITION COST & FEASIBILITY — 2 Clover Lane, Weatogue (Simsbury), CT — June 2026

For a financial advisor, the headline is this: an addition almost never "pays for itself" on paper, but it can still be the rational financial choice versus moving — because moving carries its own large, mostly-sunk transaction costs (6%+ realtor commissions, conveyance taxes, a new mortgage at ~6.5% on the full balance, and a likely 10-30% price step-up to buy a comparably larger home in Latimer Lane). The addition decision should be framed as "cost to gain X bedrooms here" vs. "net cost to gain X bedrooms by trading up," not as an ROI play.

WHAT IT COSTS PER SQUARE FOOT (Hartford County, 2025-2026):
Estimates vary widely by builder tier, so treat these as a spectrum, not a single number. Regional cost aggregators put Hartford additions at roughly $149-$298/sqft (standard to premium grade). Broader Connecticut sources cite $125-$300/sqft for basic/ground-level work and $200-$400+/sqft for complex or premium finishes. Full-service design-build firms quote materially higher — one Farmington-Valley design-build contractor cites $450-$650/sqft for turnkey custom work. Second-story additions are the most expensive per foot ($200-$500/sqft) because the existing roof must come off, the first floor must be structurally evaluated for the new load, and the family typically cannot live in the house during the most disruptive phase. New England's climate adds cost everyone underestimates: 42-inch frost-depth footings, heavy insulation, and ice-dam detailing.

For planning, I'd budget the realistic middle of this market at roughly $250-$400/sqft all-in for a quality first-floor or over-garage addition with bedrooms/bath, and $300-$500/sqft for a second-story or primary-suite addition. Use the higher end if you hire a design-build firm or want upscale finishes, and always add a 10-20% contingency on top.

CONCRETE SCENARIOS FOR THIS FAMILY (3 kids under 6 — the practical need is 1-2 more bedrooms plus a bathroom, and ideally more shared living space):
See the scenarios array for four sized options with low/high estimates. In short: a +400 sqft two-bedroom bump (over garage or first floor) runs roughly $100k-$200k; a +600 sqft primary suite + extra bedroom runs roughly $165k-$300k; a +800 sqft two-bed/bath + family-room expansion runs roughly $200k-$360k; and a full second-story addition (often +900-1,200 sqft, adding 2-3 bedrooms and a bath) runs roughly $300k-$600k. The second-story option gives the most bedrooms per dollar of land but is the most disruptive and the only one that usually forces you to move out for part of the build.

RESALE / COST-RECOUP REALITY (this matters most to your audience):
Per Remodeling's 2025 Cost vs. Value Report, additions recoup far less than kitchen/bath remodels. A midrange primary-suite addition recoups only about 27% of cost (upscale ~16%); a bathroom addition recoups about 53%; master-suite expansions nationally recoup roughly 48% by another measure. Practically: of every $1 you spend on an addition, expect to "see back" maybe $0.30-$0.55 in immediate appraised/resale value. That gap is the price of living in the space you want for the years your kids are home. The financially defensible version is to (a) keep the addition modest and bedroom/bath-focused (not a luxury primary suite), (b) make sure the finished house doesn't become the most expensive/over-improved home on the street, and (c) confirm the addition doesn't blow past what Latimer-Lane-zone comparable homes sell for, or you'll never recoup it.

TIMELINE: Plan 9-15 months door-to-door. Design + engineering 1-3 months; Simsbury permitting typically 4-12 weeks (longer if a zoning variance is needed); construction 2-3 months for a single room, 4-6 months for a multi-room first-floor/over-garage addition, and 4-8 months for a full second story. Weather (you cannot pour frost footings or open a roof mid-winter cleanly) and material lead times push real-world projects toward the long end.

SOFT COSTS (often forgotten): architect/designer $2k-$8k (more, ~8-15% of construction cost, for a full custom design-build); structural engineer / stamped plans $2k-$8k (essentially mandatory for a second story or anything altering load paths); building permits $200-$3,000 depending on valuation. Budget another 5-10% of the project for design + permitting + engineering before a single nail is driven.

SIMSBURY ZONING FEASIBILITY: See the dedicated field — the short version is that Weatogue lots are generous (mostly R-15/R-25/R-40) and setbacks are large, so a rear or over-garage addition is usually feasible by-right, but you must verify your specific lot's zone, existing setbacks, and lot coverage before assuming it.

LIVING THROUGH IT WITH THREE TODDLERS: See the dedicated note — this is a real, often-decisive factor. A first-floor or over-garage addition that you can wall off lets you stay in the home (rough, but survivable). A full second-story addition generally means renting elsewhere for 2-4 months, which adds $8k-$20k+ in rent that rarely makes it into the headline budget and should be added to your second-story numbers.

BOTTOM LINE FOR THE DECISION: If your current house is otherwise in the right neighborhood and your only gap is bedrooms, a focused +400-600 sqft addition ($100k-$300k) is very likely cheaper than the all-in cost of selling and trading up to a $700k-$900k Latimer-Lane home — even though it recoups poorly on paper — PROVIDED the addition is zoning-feasible and doesn't over-improve the property. If you also want the Latimer Lane attendance zone and 2 Clover Lane is not already in it, an addition cannot solve that, and the analysis shifts toward buy/move. (I could not independently confirm 2 Clover Lane's exact zoning district or current school attendance zone — verify both with the Town of Simsbury before deciding.)

scenarios.type: +400 sqft two-bedroom addition (over garage or first-floor rear)
scenarios.note: Adds 2 small bedrooms (or 1 bedroom + bath). Lowest-disruption option; usually fits within Weatogue setbacks by-right. HomeBlue Hartford pegs a 400 sqft (20x20) addition at $60k-$119k for basic-to-premium grade; design-build/upscale finishes push it to $150k-$200k. Best $/bedroom for staying put.

Frees up an existing bedroom for the kids by giving parents a new suite, OR adds a bedroom + bath. HomeBlue pegs 600 sqft (20x30) at $90k-$179k basic-to-premium; a true primary SUITE with bath/closet and upscale finish runs to ~$300k. Note Cost vs Value: midrange primary suite recoups only ~27%, so keep finishes restrained.

The 'do it once' option: 2 bedrooms, a bathroom, and expanded shared living space for 3 active kids. HomeBlue pegs 800 sqft (20x40) at $119k-$239k basic-to-premium; quality design-build with the extra bath and family-room glazing trends to $300k-$360k. Add 10-20% contingency.

Adds 2-3 bedrooms + bath without using yard/setback — best for tight lots or to preserve yard for kids. Most expensive per foot ($200-$500/sqft) because the roof comes off and the first floor must carry new load; nearly always requires moving out 2-4 months (add $8k-$20k+ rent). Longest timeline (4-8 months construction).

addition.resaleRecoupPct: ~27%-53% of cost recouped at resale (2025 Cost vs Value: midrange primary-suite addition ~27%, bathroom addition ~53%, national master-suite expansion ~48% by another measure). Plan on seeing roughly $0.30-$0.55 of immediate value per $1 spent; additions recoup far less than kitchen/bath remodels (~80%).
addition.typicalTimelineMonths: 9-15 months total: design/engineering 1-3 mo, Simsbury permitting ~1-3 mo (4-12+ weeks), construction 2-3 mo (single room), 4-6 mo (multi-room first-floor/over-garage), or 4-8 mo (full second story). Winter weather and material lead times push toward the long end.
addition.permitCostNote: Building permit $200-$3,000 (scales with project valuation). Add stamped structural-engineering plans $2,000-$8,000 (effectively mandatory for a second story or any load-path change). Architect/designer $2,000-$8,000 for a defined scope, or roughly 8-15% of construction cost for full design-build. Altogether budget ~5-10% of the project for design + engineering + permitting before construction. Confirm exact Simsbury permit fees with the Planning & Community Development Dept (860-658-3252).
addition.simsburyZoningFeasibility: Weatogue is served mostly by lower-density residential districts (R-15, R-25, R-40), which generally means roomy lots and large setbacks — favorable for additions. Per Simsbury's zoning regulations: R-15 requires a 15,000 sqft minimum lot, 100 ft frontage, and setbacks of ~35 ft front / 12 ft side / 25 ft rear; R-40 requires a 30,000 sqft lot, 150 ft frontage, and ~50 ft front and rear setbacks (R-25 sits between). A rear, side, or over-garage addition is usually feasible by-right if it stays within these setback lines and the lot-coverage/impervious limits. KEY FEASIBILITY CHECKS before committing: (1) confirm 2 Clover Lane's exact zoning district (verify with Simsbury Assessor/GIS); (2) measure the existing house's current distance to each property line — older homes can already be near or nonconforming to a setback, which would force a Zoning Board of Appeals variance (adds 2-4 months and approval risk); (3) confirm maximum building/impervious coverage for the lot (the published draft table showed coverage % as not-specified for some residential zones, so verify the current adopted figure with the town); (4) a second-story addition avoids setback issues entirely since it adds no footprint, but still must meet the building-height limit. A pre-application conversation with Simsbury Planning & Community Development is the cheapest way to de-risk this.
addition.livingThroughItNote: Realistic with three kids aged 3, 3, and 5 — but plan for it. A first-floor rear or over-garage addition can usually be sealed off behind a temporary wall/zip-wall, so you stay in the home: expect 4-6 months of dust, noise during nap windows, contractors arriving ~7am, a portable toilet in the driveway, loss of the garage/driveway, and intermittent shut-offs of power/water/heat. Hardest stretches are demo and the days the existing wall is opened to the new space (weather/temperature intrusion). A FULL SECOND-STORY addition is a different reality: the roof comes off, so you almost always must move out (rental or family) for ~2-4 months — budget $8k-$20k+ in rent/moving on top of construction, plus the stress of relocating toddlers' routines, sleep, and (for the 5-year-old) potentially school logistics. Given the wife works Tue/Wed/Thu, schedule the most disruptive phases mindfully and confirm contractor quiet-hours. Net: a contained first-floor/over-garage addition is the family-friendliest path to more bedrooms; reserve the second-story option for when no first-floor footprint works and you can absorb a temporary move.
addition.confidence: Medium. Cost-per-sqft ranges, Cost vs Value recoup percentages, timelines, soft costs, and Simsbury setback figures are well-supported by multiple 2025-2026 sources. Confidence is lower on (a) the exact all-in price for this specific house (depends on contractor tier, finishes, existing-structure surprises, and a site visit), (b) the precise current adopted lot-coverage % for each Simsbury residential zone (published table was ambiguous), and (c) 2 Clover Lane's specific zoning district and Latimer Lane attendance status, which I could not independently verify and which materially affect the addition-vs-move decision.
caveats: Could not independently verify 2 Clover Lane's exact zoning district, lot size, or current setback compliance — these drive whether an addition is by-right or needs a ZBA variance. Verify with Simsbury Assessor/GIS and Planning before relying on any estimate.

Could not confirm whether 2 Clover Lane is currently in the Latimer Lane Elementary attendance zone. An addition cannot change school zone; if Latimer Lane is a hard requirement and the home is not already zoned for it, that pushes the decision toward buying/moving regardless of addition economics.

Cost-per-sqft figures vary enormously by source and builder tier (regional aggregators ~$149-298/sqft vs. design-build firms $450-650/sqft). Real cost depends on finishes, site conditions, and contractor — get 2-3 itemized bids from licensed CT contractors before budgeting.

Cost vs Value recoup percentages cited mix national figures and a regional/builder-blog interpretation; the precise New England 2025 regional table sits behind JLC's paywall (HTTP 403/blocked), so New-England-specific recoup numbers could not be directly retrieved. New England recoup often runs somewhat above national averages, so treat the ~27-53% range as conservative.

Estimates exclude financing cost. An addition is typically funded via cash, HELOC, or cash-out refi at ~6.5% (Freddie Mac 30-yr avg 6.48% as of June 4, 2026); interest over the build/payback period should be added to true cost in the financial comparison.
Living-out rent for a second-story addition ($8k-$20k+) and a 10-20% construction contingency are NOT included in the headline scenario ranges and should be added.

Simsbury permit fees, current adopted lot-coverage/impervious limits per zone, and any recent (2025-2026) zoning text amendments should be confirmed directly with the Town (Planning & Community Development, 860-658-3252) as regulations were under active revision (draft changes dated Dec 2024).

sources.title: 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage Average (MORTGAGE30US) / Freddie Mac PMMS
sources.note: Mortgage-rate context for financing an addition (HELOC/cash-out refi) vs. taking a new purchase mortgage: 30-yr fixed averaged ~6.48% as of June 4, 2026 (down from 6.85% a year prior).

finance.current30yrRate: 6.48% (Freddie Mac PMMS 30-year fixed-rate average, week ending June 4, 2026, down from 6.53% the prior week). Freddie Mac chief economist Sam Khater noted affordability is "marginally improving" as rates settle in the mid-6% range. For context, the rate was 6.06% in mid-January 2026 and ~7.04% a year earlier, but climbed back after an oil/energy price shock and a stronger-than-expected May jobs report (172k jobs vs. 85k expected).
finance.current15yrRate: 5.79% (Freddie Mac PMMS 15-year fixed-rate average, week ending June 4, 2026). Roughly 0.69 percentage points below the 30-year. A 15-year loan carries a meaningfully lower rate and far less total interest, but ~50% higher monthly payment than a 30-year for the same balance, which matters for a single-strong-income household with three young kids and childcare costs.
finance.helocRate: National average HELOC rate ~7.43% (Bankrate, June 3, 2026); other surveys (Curinos/NerdWallet) show ~7.25%. Effective range across lenders ~6%-12% depending on credit, LTV, and intro promos (some teaser rates as low as 3.99%). HELOCs are variable-rate, indexed to the Wall Street Journal Prime Rate (currently ~6.5%, = Fed funds upper bound + 3.0%) plus a margin, so the rate moves with Fed policy. With the Fed on hold near 3.6% effective and the dot plot showing only modest further cuts, HELOC rates are expected to stay in the low-to-mid 7s near term. Home equity loans (fixed) average higher, ~7.86%-8.20%.
finance.tenYearTreasuryNote: ~4.52%-4.55% as of June 5, 2026 (rose ~6 bps Friday on a strong May jobs report and rising oil prices). The 10-year Treasury is the benchmark that 30-year fixed mortgage rates track most closely; the typical spread of 30-yr mortgage over the 10-yr has been historically wide (~190-200 bps here: 6.48% mortgage vs. ~4.5% Treasury), partly due to prepayment/MBS risk premiums. Watch the 10-year as the leading indicator: it would need to fall toward ~4.0% or below to drag 30-yr mortgages back toward the high-5s. Inflation reaccelerating (April CPI 3.8% YoY) and heavy Treasury supply are keeping the 10-year elevated.

rateScenarios.name: Base case (rates roughly flat, mid-6s)
rateScenarios.rationale: Aligns with the consensus of Fannie Mae (~6.3% through 2026 and most of 2027, easing toward 6.2%) and MBA (~6.4%-6.5% in 2026-2028, calling 6.5% a 'good centering point'). Assumes the Fed delivers only the ~1-2 cuts implied by the March 2026 dot plot (terminal ~3.125% by 2027-28, long-run neutral ~3.1%), inflation drifts back toward ~3% but not quickly to 2%, the 10-year holds ~4.2%-4.6%, and no recession. Most-likely planning assumption: do NOT count on dramatic rate relief. A move or refinance pencils out only if the home/space justifies a payment at ~6.3%-6.5%.

rateScenarios.name: Optimistic (rates fall)
rateScenarios.rationale: Requires inflation cooling convincingly toward 2% (the April 3.8% / near-4% spike proving transitory and energy-driven), the Fed cutting more than the dot plot shows, and/or a mild recession pushing the 10-year toward ~3.5%-4.0% (a 2027 recession is priced at ~41% odds, which would lower rates). This is the 'refinance later' upside: even a modest fall to high-5s makes a future refi or a delayed (Option C: wait) purchase materially cheaper. Note the trade-off: in Hartford's supply-starved market, falling rates would intensify bidding wars and push prices up, partly offsetting rate savings.

rateScenarios.name: Pessimistic (rates rise)
rateScenarios.rationale: Triggered by sticky/re-accelerating inflation (CPI breaching 4%, energy/oil shock persisting), the Fed forced to pause or even hike (MBA has flagged a possible 2027 hike scenario; markets already price the policy path drifting UP toward ~3.8% late-2026 and ~4.1% mid-2027), and the 10-year pushing toward 5%. In this world, the rate lock-in effect re-tightens, today's 6.48% looks attractive in hindsight, and waiting (Option C) backfires. Argues for acting sooner if a move is truly needed, and for fixed-rate (not variable HELOC) financing on any addition.

finance.rateLockInExplanation: The 'golden handcuffs' / rate lock-in effect: ~52.5% of all mortgaged U.S. homeowners still hold a rate below 4% (down from ~two-thirds in early 2022), and nearly 48% of government-backed borrowers are at or below 3.5% (Realtor.com/U.S. News data, 2026). If this family financed or refinanced 2 Clover Lane during the 2020-2021 window, they likely carry a sub-4% (possibly ~3%) 30-year mortgage. Moving means surrendering that rate and re-borrowing at ~6.48% on a larger balance. Concretely, the spread is enormous on a single payment: every $100,000 of mortgage costs roughly $420/month at 3.0% (P&I) versus roughly $632/month at 6.48% -- about $212/month, or ~$2,500/year, MORE per $100k borrowed, before even accounting for trading up to a bigger, pricier home. On a $600k loan that is well over $1,200/month of pure 'rate penalty.' This is the single biggest financial argument FOR Option A (addition) over Option B (sell/buy): an addition lets the family keep the cheap existing first mortgage intact and add space using second-lien financing (HELOC/home equity loan) or a renovation loan, rather than blowing up the whole low-rate mortgage. A cash-out refinance, by contrast, RE-PRICES the entire balance to today's ~6.5%+ and usually destroys the sub-4% advantage, so it is typically the worst option for a sub-4% holder unless they need a very large cash draw. The lock-in effect is 'easing' nationally only because the rate gap has narrowed from its peak and life events force moves -- but it has NOT disappeared, and for a sub-4% holder the math still strongly penalizes selling.

additionFinancingOptions.rateNote: ~7.25%-7.43% average, variable, tied to Prime (~6.5%) + margin; lender range ~6%-12%.
additionFinancingOptions.note: Best fit for preserving a sub-4% first mortgage. Flexible draw period (typically 10 yrs) ideal for paying contractors in stages; interest-only during draw. Downside: variable rate rises with the Fed, and the rate is ~3+ points above their likely existing first mortgage. Good for additions in the ~$50k-$200k range where the family has substantial home equity (Simsbury values up ~7% YoY).

Like a HELOC but lump-sum at a fixed rate -- removes variable-rate risk. Slightly higher rate than a HELOC today. Sensible if they want payment certainty on a known, fixed-cost addition and want to keep the low first mortgage untouched.

additionFinancingOptions.rateNote: ~6.5%-6.8% (Zillow purchase ~6.28%; cash-out carries a ~0.25%-0.50% premium over standard refi/purchase).
additionFinancingOptions.note: USUALLY THE WORST OPTION for a sub-4% holder: it re-prices the ENTIRE mortgage balance to ~6.5%+, sacrificing the pandemic-era rate. Only makes sense if (a) they have a relatively high current rate, or (b) they need a very large cash sum and value a single first-lien payment. Flag prominently in the financial analysis.

additionFinancingOptions.rateNote: Construction-to-perm ~7.25%-8.75%; standalone construction ~7.75%-9.25%; ~0.5%-1.5% above standard mortgage rates.
additionFinancingOptions.note: For a large structural addition (e.g., second-story or major footprint expansion). Single-close converts to a permanent mortgage when complete, avoiding double closing costs. Conventional renovation options also include Fannie Mae HomeStyle and Freddie Mac CHOICERenovation, which bundle the reno into one conventional mortgage. Downside: also re-prices/replaces the first mortgage, so it shares the cash-out drawback for sub-4% holders -- but is the right tool for ground-up structural work that a HELOC can't fully fund.

additionFinancingOptions.rateNote: ~6.5%-7.5% (typically ~0.75%-1.0% higher than standard FHA), PLUS 1.75% upfront MIP and ~0.55% annual MIP, plus ~1.5% supplemental origination fee.
additionFinancingOptions.note: Standard 203(k) permits structural additions (room/second-floor additions). Only 3.5% down. BUT it is a first-lien FHA product (replaces the existing mortgage, killing any sub-4% rate) and carries permanent mortgage insurance -- generally a poor fit for an existing homeowner with strong credit and equity who would do far better with a HELOC, home equity loan, or conventional HomeStyle/CHOICERenovation. Mainly relevant for low-down-payment or lower-credit buyers, which does not describe a Fidelity advisor household. Closing takes 60+ days.

finance.macroSummary: As of June 2026 the macro backdrop is 'higher-for-longer with upside inflation risk.' The effective Fed funds rate is ~3.62%, and markets price ~98.7% odds of NO change at the June 16-17, 2026 FOMC meeting. The March 2026 dot plot median showed only ~one more 25 bp cut in 2026 and one in 2027, landing near 3.125% (also the long-run neutral estimate, the highest since 2016) -- a shallow, near-finished easing cycle. The risk is to the upside: April 2026 CPI hit 3.8% YoY (highest since 2023, ~40% driven by a 17.9% energy surge), core CPI 2.8%, and analysts expected May (released June 10) to approach or exceed 4%. Futures even price the policy path drifting UP toward ~3.8% late-2026 and ~4.1% mid-2027. The 10-year Treasury sits ~4.5%-4.55% and rose on a hot May jobs report (172k vs. 85k expected). Recession odds are low near-term (~15%-30% for 2026 per NY Fed yield-curve model, prediction markets, and RSM) but rise to ~41% for 2027 (delayed weakness from elevated debt costs and >$1.3T consumer credit). Q1 2026 real GDP grew 1.6% annualized; full-year consensus ~2.0%-2.2%. Net implication for this family: do NOT bank on a sharp mortgage-rate drop; plan around ~6.3%-6.5% as the central case, with the optimistic 'refi later' path requiring inflation to break lower and/or a 2027 recession, and a real pessimistic tail where rates push toward 7%+.
finance.regionalEconomy: Connecticut / Hartford regional context (directly relevant since the husband works at Fidelity in CT and the family is anchored to Simsbury/Latimer Lane): The Hartford metro was ranked the HOTTEST U.S. housing market for 2026 by Zillow, driven by an extreme inventory shortfall (~63% below pre-pandemic levels, the most severe deficit in the country). Hartford-area home values rose 4.6% last year (fastest of any major metro) with another ~3.9% increase expected in 2026; 66.4% of Hartford homes sold above list price, the highest share in the nation. Simsbury specifically: median sale price ~$554,500 (March 2026), with Zillow's typical-value metric up ~7%-7.4% YoY -- a competitive, seller-favored market where bidding wars are common. This cuts two ways for the decision: (1) it boosts the equity available to fund an ADDITION (Option A) via HELOC/home equity loan, and (2) it makes BUYING UP (Option B) harder and more expensive -- low inventory, above-asking sales, and a $500k-$700k budget that is squarely mid-market for a larger Simsbury home, with $900k a genuine stretch. WAITING (Option C) is risky in this micro-market: prices are forecast to keep rising ~4%-10%, so any rate relief could be partly or fully offset by higher prices and stiffer competition. Labor market: CT added only ~1,200 jobs in 2025 (essentially flat), unemployment rose to 4.5% (above the ~4.3% national rate for the first time since early 2023), labor-force participation slipped, though ~70,000 job openings remain. The flat-jobs, tight-housing combination argues that the family's housing constraint is structural (supply) more than cyclical -- reinforcing that an addition or a sooner-rather-than-later move may beat waiting for a market that is unlikely to loosen quickly.
finance.narrative:
Bottom line for a financial-advisor audience weighing Addition vs. Sell/Buy vs. Wait, as of June 6, 2026:

RATES TODAY: 30-yr fixed 6.48%, 15-yr 5.79% (Freddie Mac PMMS, wk ending 6/4/26); HELOC ~7.25%-7.43% (variable, Prime-linked); cash-out refi ~6.5%-6.8%; construction-to-perm ~7.25%-8.75%; FHA 203(k) ~6.5%-7.5% plus MIP. The 10-yr Treasury (~4.5%) and reaccelerating inflation (April CPI 3.8%) are keeping mortgage rates sticky in the mid-6s.

THE LOCK-IN MATH IS THE CRUX. If this family holds a pandemic-era sub-4% mortgage (statistically likely -- 52.5% of mortgaged owners do), then Option B (sell/buy) and any cash-out refinance force them to re-price their ENTIRE balance from ~3% to ~6.5%+ -- roughly +$212/month per $100k borrowed -- ON TOP of trading into a larger, pricier Simsbury home (median ~$554k, market up ~7% YoY, often selling above asking). That double penalty (lost low rate + higher price + bigger loan) is the strongest financial case FOR Option A (addition), because an addition preserves the cheap first mortgage and finances the new space with a second lien (HELOC or fixed home equity loan at ~7.25%-8.2%) or a renovation loan. A HELOC/home equity loan on their built-up Simsbury equity is almost always cheaper, all-in, than re-mortgaging the whole house. Reserve cash-out refi, construction-to-perm, and FHA 203(k) for cases needing large/structural funding -- and note 203(k) is generally a poor fit for a high-credit, high-equity Fidelity-advisor household (it kills the low rate and adds permanent MIP).

RATE OUTLOOK / TIMING (Option C, wait): Consensus (Fannie Mae ~6.3%, MBA ~6.4%-6.5%) sees rates essentially FLAT in the mid-6s through 2026-2028, with the Fed nearly done cutting (dot-plot terminal ~3.1%) and inflation risk skewed UP. So 'waiting for rates to fall' is a weak base case -- meaningful relief (high-5s) needs inflation to break lower or a 2027 recession (~41% odds). And even if rates fall, Hartford/Simsbury's severe supply shortage (#1 hottest U.S. market, inventory ~63% below normal, prices forecast +4% to +10%) means lower rates would likely bid prices UP, eroding the savings. Waiting is therefore higher-risk in this specific micro-market than nationally.

RECOMMENDED FRAMING: Lead with Option A (addition financed via second-lien, preserving the low first mortgage) as the rate-efficient choice; treat Option B as justified only if the addition can't deliver the needed bedrooms/Latimer Lane zoning or if their existing rate isn't actually low; treat Option C (wait) as the weakest, since neither rates nor inventory are forecast to improve materially and prices keep climbing. Any addition financing should favor FIXED-rate structures given the upside inflation/rate risk; a variable HELOC is fine for staged draws but should be paid down or refinanced to fixed if the rate path rises.
finance.confidence: Medium-high. Current rate levels (30-yr 6.48%, 15-yr 5.79%, HELOC ~7.4%, 10-yr ~4.5%, Fed funds ~3.62%, CPI 3.8%) are sourced from primary/authoritative June 2026 releases (Freddie Mac PMMS, Bankrate, FRED-tracked Treasury, BLS CPI). Forward forecasts (Fannie Mae, MBA, dot plot) are inherently uncertain and were revised upward after a 2026 energy/inflation shock, so the rate-scenario bands are deliberately wide. The lock-in math assumes -- but cannot confirm -- that this family holds a sub-4% mortgage; that should be verified from their actual loan documents. Simsbury-specific and Latimer-Lane-zone pricing carries the most uncertainty (small market, limited granular data).
caveats: KEY ASSUMPTION TO VERIFY: The entire lock-in / 'golden handcuffs' argument hinges on the family actually holding a sub-4% mortgage. Confirm their current rate, balance, and remaining term from their loan statement before relying on the Option A vs. B comparison.

May 2026 CPI was not yet released as of this research (due June 10, 2026); analysts expected it to approach or exceed 4%. If it prints hot, the pessimistic rate scenario gains weight and forecasts could be revised upward again.
The June 16-17, 2026 FOMC meeting and updated dot plot had not occurred at research time; figures rely on the March 2026 SEP and market pricing. A hawkish June surprise would shift the trajectory.

Forecasts already proved volatile in 2026: a late-Feb energy/geopolitical shock pushed the 30-yr from ~5.98% to ~6.46% within weeks and caused Fannie Mae to revise its forecast from ~5.7% (Q4 2026) up to ~6.3%. Treat all forward rates as scenario bands, not point estimates.

HELOC rates are variable and Prime-linked; the ~7.25%-7.43% quoted will move with future Fed decisions. Quoted lender ranges (e.g., 3.99% low end) are often promotional teaser rates, not the ongoing rate.
Construction-to-perm, cash-out refi, and FHA 203(k) figures are national ranges; CT/local lender quotes will vary with credit, LTV, and project scope and should be obtained directly.

Simsbury median prices and the 'Hartford #1 hottest market' ranking are metro/town-level; no street-level Latimer-Lane-zone price data was verifiable. Confirm with a local agent or the Simsbury assessor/MLS.
Some current-rate figures vary slightly by source/methodology and time of day (e.g., 10-yr 4.48%-4.55%; HELOC 7.25% vs 7.43%); ranges are noted where they exist.
This is general financial/market information, not personalized mortgage or investment advice; actual loan eligibility and pricing depend on the family's full financial profile.

forecast.narrative:
FORWARD-LOOKING INVENTORY & PRICE FORECAST — LATIMER LANE ZONE / SIMSBURY, CT (as of June 6, 2026)

CONTEXT FOR THE DECISION. Your current home at 2 Clover Lane, Weatogue is already inside the Latimer Lane Elementary attendance zone (Clover Lane is described by listing agents as "in the heart of the Latimer Lane Elementary School District," and Latimer Lane School is at 33 Mountain View Rd., Weatogue 06089). That is decision-relevant: Option A (addition) keeps you in the zone you already want; Options B and C require buying back into the SAME zone you already occupy, paying full transaction + price-appreciation costs to do so. The inventory question is really "how often, and at what price, does a larger Latimer-zone house come available?"

1) TURNOVER / VELOCITY (how often homes trade).
Simsbury is a low-velocity, supply-starved market. Town-wide, roughly 225 homes of all types sold in the trailing 12 months (Homes.com); single-family closings run lower, on the order of ~170-200/yr. Monthly cadence is thin: ~34 sales in March 2026 (vs 31 a year prior), 16-17 single-family closings in May 2026, and YTD Jan-May 2026 single-family sales of 69 units, actually DOWN 4.2% vs the same period in 2025. Against ~9,358 occupied units (75.2% owner-occupied ≈ ~7,000 owner households), ~200 sales/yr is an annual turnover rate of only ~2.5-3% — i.e., the typical owner-occupied house changes hands roughly once every 30+ years at current velocity. The Latimer Lane zone is one of three elementary zones (with Squadron Line and Tariffville/Tootin' Hills) and covers a slice of southern Simsbury/Weatogue, so a rough zone-level estimate is on the order of ~50-80 single-family sales per year town-wide attributable to the Latimer area, of which only a fraction are the 4+ bedroom larger homes you'd be targeting. Practically: expect a small handful (single digits) of genuinely-larger Latimer-zone homes in your $500-700k (stretch $900k) range to hit the market in any given year. Inventory is currently very tight: ~35-50 active listings town-wide at any time, ~14-18 days on market, and homes selling at or ABOVE asking (William Pitt reports ~113% of asking in May 2026, average sale ~$773k).

2) MEDIAN OWNERSHIP TENURE.
No Simsbury-specific tenure figure is published, but the velocity math above (≈30-year implied holding period) and Connecticut's status as the nation's most supply-constrained market both point to tenure WELL ABOVE the U.S. median of 12 years (2025, Redfin). Long tenure = strong "lock-in" + lifestyle inertia = slow natural turnover. This is the single biggest structural reason inventory in this zone stays scarce.

3) "SILVER TSUNAMI" LATENT SUPPLY.
The demographics are favorable to future listings on a multi-year horizon: 17.1% of Simsbury residents are 65+ and 29.4% are 55+ (ACS 2017-2021), with median age ~42-46 and rising. A large cohort of long-tenured empty-nesters in big colonials is the natural future seller pool as they downsize/age out. BUT the timing is unpredictable and gradual — aging owners in CT are staying put longer (low senior mobility, limited local downsizing product, property-tax/Medicaid considerations), so this is a slow tailwind to supply over 3-5+ years, not a near-term wave. It modestly favors WAITING if you can tolerate price risk, and it is the demographic case that more inventory eventually comes.

4) NEW CONSTRUCTION PIPELINE (supply that could be ADDED to the zone).
- LARGEST item: "The Ridge at Talcott Mountain South," the former Hartford Insurance campus at 200 Hopmeadow St (southern Simsbury/Weatogue area — relevant to the Latimer side of town). Revised 8-30g plan ≈ 669-689 units (≈596-616 apartments, 54 duplex units in 27 buildings, only ~19 single-family homes; 30% / ~201 units affordable). STATUS IS HIGHLY UNCERTAIN AND CONTESTED: a prior 432-unit version was rejected by Zoning in 2024, the developer sued and the town prevailed, and as of May 2026 it was back in wetlands public hearings (continued to May 19, 2026). If it ever proceeds, it is overwhelmingly apartments/duplexes (rental/attached), would build out in phases over years, and adds very few detached single-family homes — so it does little to ease the specific 4-bedroom single-family supply you want, though it could pressure local sentiment and traffic.
- Small for-sale subdivisions: 40 Holcomb St — 4-lot subdivision APPROVED 7-0 (May 2025); 152 Old Farms Rd — 7-lot re-subdivision PROPOSED (R-80); Copper Hill Estates (formerly Cambridge Crossing) on Cambridge Ct off Hoskins Rd — site-plan amendment in process. These add only a few dozen new single-family lots town-wide over multiple years.
NET: New construction will NOT meaningfully relieve the larger-single-family shortage in the Latimer zone in the next 1-3 years. Most pipeline volume is contested multifamily; the for-sale pieces are tiny.

5) INTEREST-RATE PATH → SUPPLY (the lock-in mechanism).
The 30-yr fixed averaged 6.48% (Freddie Mac, June 4 2026). Consensus forecasts: ~6.2-6.5% through 2026 (Freddie/Fannie/MBA), ~5.8-6.2% in 2027, possibly ~5.5-5.9% by 2028. Mechanism: as rates fall toward the rates existing owners already carry, the financial penalty of giving up a low note shrinks, "lock-in" melts, and listings rise. Nationally that thaw is underway — by early 2026 more owners hold rates ABOVE 6% than below 3%, and U.S. resale inventory is ~20% above a year ago. CRITICAL CAVEAT: CONNECTICUT IS LAGGING THAT THAW. Statewide active listings were still DOWN ~4.8% YoY in April 2026 (≈10,003 homes) while prices rose ~10%+ YoY. CT remains the most supply-constrained state in the country. So the rate-driven supply relief that's helping national buyers has NOT yet shown up in Simsbury, and may arrive later and weaker here.

6) OUTLOOK BY HORIZON (reasoned forecast, not a guarantee).
- 1-2 YEARS: Inventory stays TIGHT. Rates only ease modestly; CT's lock-in barely loosens; pipeline adds little for-sale single-family supply. Expect continued low double-digit active listings, fast sales, at/above asking. Prices keep rising mid-single-digits (~3-7%/yr) town-wide, possibly higher for scarce larger Latimer-zone homes. Few larger homes will list; competition will be stiff.
- 3 YEARS: GRADUAL LOOSENING. If rates drift toward high-5s/low-6s, some lock-in releases and early silver-tsunami listings begin; small subdivisions deliver lots; the Hartford campus project may or may not be resolved. Inventory likely modestly better but still a seller-leaning market. Price growth decelerates toward inflation-plus (~2-4%/yr) but is unlikely to fall.
- 5 YEARS: STRUCTURALLY MORE SUPPLY, BUT GROWTH-CONSTRAINED. The 55+/65+ cohort increasingly lists; rates likely lower; more attached/multifamily exists in town. Best odds of a more balanced market and a real selection of larger Latimer-zone homes — but Simsbury's desirability, top schools, and CT's chronic underbuilding mean a true buyer's market and price declines are NOT the base case. Real (inflation-adjusted) prices may flatten; nominal prices likely still grind upward.

DECISION IMPLICATION (inventory/price lens only; financial-advisor audience can layer in carrying-cost/transaction math). The thing you want — a larger home in the exact zone you already live in — is structurally scarce and likely to STAY scarce for 1-3 years, with selection improving only gradually by year 3-5. Waiting (Option C) is a bet that (a) more inventory appears as rates fall and seniors downsize and (b) prices don't run away first; the evidence says inventory relief in CT will be slow and prices are still rising ~6-10%/yr now, so waiting carries real price/affordability risk and uncertain inventory payoff. Building an addition (Option A) is the only option that does not require winning a low-supply bidding war to stay in the Latimer zone, and it hedges the inventory scarcity entirely — its case rests on construction cost vs. the move-up price gap + ~5-6% transaction frictions of buying-and-selling in this hot, low-inventory market. Selling/buying now (Option B) means transacting at the top of a seller's market into thin inventory — feasible but you'll likely pay up and have little selection.

income.narrative:
HOUSEHOLD INCOME & TAX CONTEXT (for the financial-advisor audience). All figures are estimates triangulated from public salary aggregators and town/state sources as of mid-2026; treat them as ranges, not the family's actual numbers.

1) FIDELITY FINANCIAL ADVISOR / CONSULTANT (Connecticut). Fidelity's branch "advisor" track spans a wide range, so the right number depends heavily on the husband's specific role. Comp structure (per Fidelity's representative-compensation disclosure): a fixed base salary PLUS an annual variable bonus that is a percentage of base, set by manager assessment of performance against client- and business-objectives (not a pure commission/AUM grid). Roles, low to high: (a) Investor Center entry roles -- Investment Consultant / Financial Consultant Program: CT averages ~$98,800/yr, with the middle of the market ~$75,200 (25th pct) to ~$118,900 (75th pct) and ~$133,700 at the 90th pct (ZipRecruiter, CT). Glassdoor's national Financial Consultant figure is ~$73K base + ~$18K additional (~$91K total) at the junior end. (b) Branch Financial Advisor (CT): ~$97,000/yr average, ~$71,300-$124,600 interquartile, ~$130,300 at 90th pct; higher-cost metros like Stamford run ~$109,000. (c) Senior / dedicated / High-Net-Worth or Private-Wealth advisors: nationally Fidelity Financial Advisor TOTAL compensation (base+variable) averages ~$201,000, with a typical band of ~$151,000-$281,000 and top earners ~$368,000 (PayScale/Glassdoor national). Net: a reasonable planning range for an established CT Fidelity advisor is roughly $110,000 on the low/Investor-Center end to $250,000+ for a tenured dedicated/HNW advisor; we use ~$130,000-$220,000 as the central planning band, flagging that you (the advisor) know the exact base + bonus and should substitute it.

2) PUBLIC-SCHOOL SLP, PART-TIME ~0.6 FTE (CT). Full-time CT school SLP salary: median ~$105,800/yr, interquartile ~$97,700-$113,400, 10th-90th pct ~$90,300-$120,250 (Salary.com, "School Speech Language Pathologist, CT," June 2026). A second Salary.com benchmark for SLP in CT shows a ~$104,000 median; ZipRecruiter's "School Speech Pathologist CT" runs lower (~$82,000-$99,000 average), reflecting methodology/experience mix. Taking a full-time central value of ~$95,000-$110,000 and scaling to 0.6 FTE (works Tue/Wed/Thu = 3 of 5 days) yields roughly $57,000-$66,000; with benefits/step-scale variation, plan on ~$55,000-$70,000. Note: public-school SLPs are typically on a district step-and-lane salary schedule with a state pension (CT Teachers' Retirement) and a ~10-month work year, so the part-time figure is fairly stable and predictable -- a point in favor of debt-service reliability.

3) COMBINED HOUSEHOLD INCOME. Low/conservative: ~$130,000 (advisor, Investor-Center end) + ~$55,000 (SLP 0.6 FTE) = ~$185,000. High/established: ~$220,000 (tenured advisor) + ~$70,000 = ~$290,000. We therefore model a household gross income range of roughly $185,000-$290,000, with a central planning figure near $230,000-$250,000. For sanity, Simsbury's own median household income is ~$162,000 (ZIP 06070, 2024) and West Simsbury ~$201,000, so this household is at or above the town median -- consistent with carrying a Simsbury home.

4) SIMSBURY PROPERTY TAX. Current mill rate (2024 Grand List, billed FY2025-26): 33.02 mills (town) + 1.14 mills (fire district) = 34.16 total on real estate/personal property; motor-vehicle is capped at 32.46. The Board of Selectmen's FY2026-27 budget proposes raising the town real-estate rate to 33.74 mills (ex-fire). HOW CT PROPERTY TAX IS COMPUTED: Connecticut assesses property at 70% of market (appraised) value, then multiplies the assessed value by the mill rate (1 mill = $1 of tax per $1,000 of assessed value). Formula: annual tax = (market value x 0.70 / 1,000) x mill rate. Using the combined ~34.16 mills: a $500,000 home -> $350,000 assessed -> ~$11,956/yr; a $700,000 home -> $490,000 assessed -> ~$16,738/yr; a $900,000 home -> $630,000 assessed -> ~$21,521/yr. (At the proposed 33.74 town-only rate plus 1.14 fire = 34.88, those rise ~2%.) This tax is a material monthly line item -- roughly $1,000/mo at $500k up to ~$1,800/mo at $900k -- and must be added to P&I and insurance in any affordability test.

5) AFFORDABILITY / DTI NOTE (rough). Lenders generally target a housing (front-end) ratio of ~28% of gross monthly income and a total-debt (back-end) ratio of ~36-43%. At the central household income of ~$230,000 gross (~$19,170/mo): 28% front-end = ~$5,370/mo for PITI; a 36% back-end (less existing debts like car/student loans) = ~$6,900/mo total. At the conservative $185,000 ($15,420/mo): 28% = ~$4,320/mo PITI. So total monthly housing capacity is roughly $4,300-$5,400/mo at 28% front-end across the income range. After backing out Simsbury property tax (~$1,000-$1,800/mo) and homeowners insurance (~$150-$250/mo), the remaining room for principal+interest is roughly $3,000-$4,000/mo. At ~6.5-7% 30-yr rates that P&I supports a mortgage of very roughly $450,000-$600,000, which -- with a typical down payment -- aligns well with the stated $500k-$700k comfortable budget and confirms the ~$900k "real stretch" label (a $900k purchase would push PITI toward/above the 28% line unless the down payment is large or income is at the high end). Bottom line for the decision: the family appears comfortably able to carry a $500k-$700k Latimer Lane-zone home; $900k is feasible only at the upper income scenario and/or with a substantial down payment, and Simsbury's ~34-mill tax adds meaningfully to monthly cost at every price point.

model.biggestFinancialDriver: The mortgage-rate lock-in effect (the "golden handcuffs"). If the family holds a pandemic-era sub-4% first mortgage (modeled at 3.25%, statistically likely since they refinanced/bought in the 2020-2021 window and 52.5% of mortgaged U.S. owners still hold sub-4% rates), then EVERY dollar of new debt is the swing variable. The arithmetic: at 3.25% a $100k of mortgage costs ~$435/mo P&I; at today's 6.48% it costs ~$632/mo -- a +$197/mo (~$2,400/yr) "rate penalty" per $100k borrowed, before any price step-up. Moving (Option B/C) re-prices the ENTIRE balance to ~6.5%, while an addition (Option A) preserves the cheap first mortgage and only borrows the new ~$305k of project cost at ~7.75% via a second lien. Because of this, the 10-year CASH cost of all three options lands within a tight band (~$505k-$536k for the locked holder) -- the lock-in is what makes building competitive with, rather than far worse than, moving. This single assumption (their actual current rate and balance) must be verified from the loan statement before trusting the ranking; if their rate is NOT low (modeled current-rate scenario), the addition's cost advantage shrinks and moving becomes relatively more attractive.
model.recommendationPreview: Lean Option A (a focused ~600-800 sqft addition, financed by a fixed home-equity loan that preserves the low first mortgage) -- with the decisive caveat that 2 Clover Lane is ALREADY in the Latimer Lane Elementary zone, so moving buys no school upgrade. The addition wins on the metrics that matter to a financial-advisor household: it keeps a sub-4% first mortgage intact, avoids ~$47k-$52k of sunk transaction friction, keeps a low ~$284k tax basis (a move resets to ~$455k assessed = +$6,000/yr tax), and ends 10 years with the highest equity and home value of the three. Option B (move now) is justified only if the addition is zoning-infeasible, the family wants turnkey space immediately, or their current rate is NOT actually low. Option C (wait) is the weakest: it sits between A and B on cost but bets on rate/inventory relief that the consensus (Fannie ~6.3%, MBA ~6.4-6.5%) and Connecticut's #1-hottest, supply-starved market do not support -- prices are forecast to keep rising 4-10%/yr, likely outrunning any rate savings.
model.narrative:
FRAMING & THE DECISIVE NON-FINANCIAL FACT. Before any math: 2 Clover Lane is ALREADY in the Latimer Lane Elementary attendance zone (Simsbury Public Schools street listing, rev. 4/23/2025 lists "Clover Lane -- Latimer"; Latimer is the town's #1-rated elementary, GreatSchools 9/10, U.S. News #33 in CT). The family's stated #2 priority -- "being in the Latimer Lane zone" -- is already satisfied at zero cost. Moving therefore delivers NO school upgrade (at best a lateral move to Tootin' Hills, at worst a slight step down). That collapses the decision to a pure space-vs-money question and is the strongest qualitative argument for staying (Option A or C-stay).

SHARED ASSUMPTIONS (all explicit; substitute the family's real numbers): current home market value $575,000 (blended from the $546k Zillow/$618k Movoto range); current first mortgage modeled from a 12/2020 purchase at $339,000 with 20% down ($271,200 loan) at a locked 3.25%, ~5.5 yrs amortized = ~$239,000 remaining balance and ~$336,000 equity today; "current rate" scenario uses 6.5% (Freddie Mac 6.48%, wk 6/4/26); home-equity (2nd-lien) financing at 7.75% fixed/20yr; nominal home appreciation 4%/yr (conservative vs Simsbury's reported +5.9% to +16.4% YoY); Simsbury 2025 mill rate 35.00 on a 70% assessment; insurance ~$2,400/yr; maintenance 1%/yr of value; selling cost 6%, buying closing 2%; new target home $650,000 (e.g., the live, in-zone match at 5 Lawton Dr: 4bd/3,157sqft/1.07ac/$649,900).

OPTION A -- ADDITION. Scope a ~700 sqft "do-it-once" addition (2 bedrooms + bath + family-room expansion): base $250,000 + 15% contingency ($37,500) + ~7% soft costs (design/engineering/permits, $17,500) = ~$305,000 all-in. Financed via a fixed home-equity loan ($305k @ 7.75%/20yr = ~$2,504/mo), keeping the 3.25% first mortgage ($1,180/mo P&I) untouched. Upfront cash: ~$0 (financed). Resulting total debt: ~$544,000. Addition raises assessed value ~$140k -> property tax rises to ~$14,900/yr. MONTHLY (LOCKED): first $1,180 + HE $2,504 + tax $1,238 + ins $200 = ~$5,122. MONTHLY (CURRENT-RATE, i.e., if their first were instead 6.5%): ~$5,453. 10-YR CASH COST (interest + tax + ins + maint, locked): ~$536,000. EQUITY @10yr: ~$775,000 on a ~$1,147,000 home (4bd colonial expanded to ~2,700 sqft on 1.59 acres). NET (equity minus 10yr cash cost): ~$239,000 -- the HIGHEST of the three, driven by ending with the most valuable asset and keeping the cheap first mortgage. Caveat: additions recoup poorly on resale (~27-53% per 2025 Cost vs Value), so the spend is "buying years of livable space," not an ROI play; keep finishes restrained so the house doesn't over-improve the street. Zoning is favorable (1.59 ac vs 40,000 sqft R-40 minimum) but the 40-ft side-yard setback means a REAR addition is the feasible path -- confirm exact setbacks via survey/GIS before scoping.

OPTION B -- MOVE NOW. Sell $575k home (-6% costs $34,500 = $540,500 proceeds), pay off $239k loan -> ~$301,000 equity freed. Buy $650,000 (+2% closing $13,000); apply equity as ~$288,000 down (44%) -> new loan ~$362,000. UPFRONT SUNK transaction cost: ~$47,500. New tax resets to 70% of $650k = $455k assessed -> ~$15,925/yr (about +$6,000/yr vs today). MONTHLY (the REALITY of moving, new loan @6.5%): P&I $2,285 + tax $1,327 + ins $200 = ~$3,812. The "locked" column for B is a counterfactual (~$3,100) showing the lost benefit -- moving forces a market-rate loan, so $3,812 is the operative figure. 10-YR CASH COST (incl sunk txn, @6.5%): ~$528,000. EQUITY @10yr: ~$656,000 on a $962,000 home. NET: ~$128,000. The lower monthly than Option A is misleading: it reflects a SMALLER total debt and a smaller/less-valuable end asset; B's net wealth position trails A by ~$110k largely because of the forfeited low rate plus ~$47.5k of friction, and it secures no school improvement.

OPTION C -- WAIT ~2 YEARS THEN MOVE. Carry the current home 2 more years (locked-rate carry cost ~$51,000 in interest+tax+ins+maint), during which the target home rises at ~5%/yr to ~$717,000 while the current home rises at 4%/yr to ~$622,000. Then transact at an assumed ~6.25% (base-case rates stay mid-6s): sell -6%, pay off ~$226k, ~$359k equity freed, down ~$344k (48%), new loan ~$372,000; sunk txn ~$51,600; new tax ~$17,557/yr. MONTHLY after move (@6.25%): ~$3,955. 10-YR CASH COST: ~$505,000 (lowest headline, because 2 of the 10 years are spent in the cheap current home before re-pricing). EQUITY @10yr: ~$652,000 on a $981,000 home. NET: ~$148,000. C's modestly lower 10-yr cash cost is fragile: it depends on rates easing slightly AND prices growing "only" 5%/yr -- but the research shows Hartford is the #1 hottest U.S. market with inventory ~63% below normal and CT lagging the national inventory thaw, so the realistic risk is that prices outrun any rate relief, larger in-zone homes stay scarce (only a handful list per year, selling at ~113% of asking in 14 days), and the family pays MORE for LESS choice later.

BOTTOM LINE. For a sub-4% locked holder, 10-yr cash costs cluster tightly ($505k-$536k), so the decision turns on (1) ending net wealth/asset value -- where A leads by ~$90-110k -- and (2) the already-secured Latimer zone, which removes the main reason to move. A is the rate-efficient, school-secure choice provided the addition is zoning-feasible and not over-built. B makes sense only if construction is infeasible/undesirable or their current rate isn't actually low. C is the weakest: it bets against a consensus that says neither rates nor inventory improve materially, while prices keep climbing.
